home.name: Dorian
home.tagline: I design and build modern web experiences, from front-end to back-end.
home.featured_subtitle: A selection of what I've built recently.

about.title: About
about.subtitle: Presentation, skills, background…
about.intro: Everyone calls me Dodo. Not just because it's been my nickname forever, but because I genuinely relate to the bird. The dodo is warm, endearing, one of a kind. And in a way, that's kind of me. This site is a window into my life - to get to know me and see what I do.
about.declic: Ever since I was a kid, I've been curious about everything. For a long time, it was architecture that made me dream - designing spaces, building things that last. But alongside that, without really noticing, I was just as drawn to computers: PCs, video games, understanding how things work behind the screen.
about.declic_2: My 9th-grade internship at an architecture firm changed everything. Living the job day-to-day, I realized the reality didn't match what I'd imagined. The spark was almost instant: tech had never really let me go. The answer had been there all along.
about.school: At a student fair, I met Aurore. She was representing MyDigitalSchool, and she introduced the school in a way that spoke to me immediately - no rehearsed speech, just an honest and convincing presentation. That day, she gave me the confidence I needed to take the leap.
about.school_2: Since joining MyDigitalSchool, zero regrets. I found an environment that fits me, real-world projects, amazing people, and above all the confirmation that web development was truly my path.
about.learning: What I love about development is that exact moment when you're stuck on a problem. You rack your brain, try a solution, it doesn't work, you try again differently, and then… it works. I never get tired of that feeling.
about.learning_2: But I'm not the type to stay in my corner either. I like to exchange ideas, understand people's needs, explain my solutions in simple terms. Being useful to others, whether through code or through a conversation, matters a lot to me.
about.equans: My real transition into the professional world came through my internship at Equans Digital. From March to June 2025, I joined the Data team within the Application Solutions group. I worked on WR2GO, an internal web app for energy data visualization - fixing bugs, adding features, maintaining consistency between Angular and Spring Boot.
about.equans_2: What struck me most was something my mentor said during my final presentation: 'Dorian was profitable for us, he saved us money.' Coming from a professional in a formal evaluation, it meant more to me than any grade ever could. That's when I decided to ask to continue as an apprentice - and they said yes.
about.alternance: Since September 2025, I've been an apprentice at Equans Digital alongside my second year of Bachelor's at MyDigitalSchool. It's a natural continuation: same team, same environment, and every week helps me grow both technically and personally.
about.workflow: I've developed two distinct approaches. On my personal projects, I go with a structured instinct: I build 'to-do' lists that I adjust as I make progress. But in the professional world, like at Equans, I fully blend into the strict Agile methodology and methodically clear the tickets assigned to me.
about.workflow_2: When facing a stubborn bug, I always start by trying to logically figure out what's broken. If I hit a wall, I don't hesitate to ask my colleagues for help, or to use AI as a 'pair-programmer' on personal setups. And if the problem is really tough? I know when to pause. Stepping away from my desk, walking, and getting a breath of fresh air for five minutes—disconnecting is often when the solution finally pops up.
about.workflow_3: In a team, my role completely adapts to the context. At school, I naturally tend to step up as an organizer and take on responsibilities. However, at my current company, fully aware of my apprentice status and the expertise of those around me, I position myself as a 'sponge': I listen closely and follow directions carefully to absorb as many best practices as possible.
about.outside: Big fan of video games (Rocket League, League of Legends) and sports - football holds a special place, especially GF38, my local club since forever. I love pushing myself, whether on a pitch, at the gym, or against a stubborn bug at 11 PM.
about.outside_2: On the engagement side, I'm vice-president of my school's student union and a MyDigitalSchool ambassador. Representing my school and bringing students together is my way of giving back a little of what it gave me.
about.outside_3: My friends would say I'm kind, funny, and always there when needed. The nickname Dodo comes from Dorian, but it's always stuck way beyond that. I naturally identified with the bird: warm, endearing, a bit unique in its own way.
about.future: My journey isn't very long yet, but every step has confirmed I'm in the right place. Going forward, I see myself continuing to grow as a fullstack web developer, building projects with real impact, and keeping that connection with people - talking, exchanging, understanding needs. Project manager, tech consultant, or something else entirely: I don't know exactly, and I'm fine with that.
about.future_cta: If you're here, maybe we have something to build together.

projects.subtitle: Discover my work, from code to mockups.

test.title: Test Project
test.short: A test project for demonstration purposes.
test.desc: This is a sample project description for testing and demonstration.

super-todo.title: Superb Todo List
super-todo.short: A complete task management app with priorities, deadlines, and dark mode.
super-todo.desc: A personal task management app built with Vue 3. A project born from the curiosity to test a third front-end framework alongside React and Angular, and the desire to create a practical organization tool that I actually use daily.
super-todo.context: The initiative for this project was primarily personal and started from a simple observation. In class, we had learned to use React, and during my apprenticeship at Equans Digital, I work daily with Angular. However, I kept hearing about Vue.js, often touted as the most accessible and logical framework to start with. Rather than just taking people's word for it, I wanted to form my own opinion. The idea was to tackle a classic project—the famous 'Todo List'—but push it far enough to really explore this third ecosystem and complete my learning of the front-end framework "trinity".
super-todo.objective: From the very beginning, I didn't want to build just another throwaway coding exercise or tech demo. My goal was to create a tool I would genuinely use to organize myself. For that to happen, the app had to be polished, clear, and most importantly, accessible from anywhere. That's why I planned for a Vercel deployment with the specific intent of pinning it to my smartphone's home screen as a shortcut. This meant it needed flawless responsive design, proper priority management, clear visual deadlines so nothing slips through the cracks, and a dark mode for comfortable daily use.
super-todo.approach: The real challenge wasn't hitting insurmountable technical walls, but rather understanding and absorbing Vue's specific philosophy. I took the time to study the Composition API (using <script setup>) and compared its reactive state management with what I already knew from Angular and React Hooks. I structured the application into single-responsibility components. For data persistence—crucial for daily use without a backend—I implemented local saving via LocalStorage. Finally, I added details like internationalization and smooth TransitionGroup animations to make the interactions feel alive.
features: Priority management (high, medium, low) with smart sorting
Deadlines with red visual alert when overdue
Dark/light mode with user preference persistence
Internationalization (French/English) including priority badges
Inline editing of text and date directly in the list
Responsive interface with smooth animations (TransitionGroup)
super-todo.learnings: This project allowed me to confirm one thing: Vue.js fully deserves its reputation for having a smooth learning curve. I was particularly impressed by the template directive system (the famous @click, v-model, v-if), which makes the HTMl code highly readable and almost natural. However, through this full-scale test, I also realized that tool preference is heavily tied to habit. Despite Vue's appealing simplicity, if I had to rebuild this project today, I would most likely go with React, where I've developed more muscle memory and comfort. Regardless, the experiment was a success: the app sits on my phone and I actually use it!

50ans.title: Event Site - 50th Birthday
50ans.short: Mini event site for Karyn's 50th birthday: event info, online registration and guest list.
50ans.desc: My very first website ever deployed, and more importantly, my first "client" relationship without a safety net. A seemingly simple project (registrations and practical info) that laid the foundation for how I approach deployment and user experience.
50ans.context: This project was born out of a very concrete need: my stepmother wanted something memorable for her 50th birthday, going well beyond a basic WhatsApp or Facebook group. So, I offered to develop a dedicated website during my free time. Beyond the fun aspect, it was my first real experience with a "client": even though she was family, there were real requirements, a need for follow-ups, and a deliverable to secure. That completely shifted the stakes compared to a standard school project.
50ans.objective: The major challenge was accessibility. My stepmother's goal was to send the "bare" link via SMS, without having to explain how the website worked. Right from the landing, the user had to instinctively understand that they needed to register or that they could check the event's details. I therefore needed an incredibly clear interface, designed for mobile, with massive call-to-action buttons and visual tricks (like a little 'shake' effect on the registration button) to naturally guide the eye.
50ans.approach: The technical approach perfectly reflects my early days. I used the classic arsenal: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for the front-end, with PHP to handle the registration and basically store it in a JSON file (using MAMP locally). While the coding itself was fairly straightforward, the real wall was the deployment. I had never put a website online. I had to get a PlanetHoster account through my school and spent hours searching for configuration solutions, reading documentation, and watching tutorials to link my local code to this remote server.
features: Homepage with dynamic JS countdown and quick links
Registration form with PHP processing and JSON storage
Dynamically updated guest list page
Practical info: venue, schedule, dress code, accommodation
Background animation (stars / snow effect in JS)
Festive, responsive design for mobile and desktop
50ans.learnings: Looking back, there's a highly amusing side to this project. If I had to redo it today, I would probably use headless tools like Supabase or Strapi for the backend, just to save an insane amount of time. But the biggest lesson—and what gives this first project its charm—is that I developed and deployed it entirely without using Git or GitHub, because I simply hadn't studied them yet! It reminds me of how much I've evolved since, both in terms of versioning best practices and the ease of modern deployment.

stegano.title: Stegano Lab
stegano.short: Educational web app about steganography: course, progressive hands-on lab, LSB encode/decode tools, and quiz assessment.
stegano.desc: Stegano Lab is a Learning Lab project built at MyDigitalSchool Grenoble. The goal was to make steganography concrete and accessible through a complete 4-part flow (course, lab, tools, assessment), with a deliberately readable LSB implementation designed for classroom use.
stegano.context: This project came from a real personal spark. I discovered steganography in a YouTube video by "What a Fail", and the idea of hiding a message inside an image immediately caught my attention. I quickly saw how this principle connects to broader topics like cybersecurity, information discretion, and even the spy culture often seen in digital content. At the same time, as part of MyDigitalSchool Grenoble, we had to deliver a Learning Lab project with three mandatory parts: a course, a practical lab, and an evaluation. I therefore chose to introduce steganography to the class with a simple, concrete, and progressive approach. There was also a strong third motivation: seriously testing what I could produce with AI on a full project, in real conditions, while still keeping full technical ownership of the final outcome.
stegano.objective: The expected result was a complete educational web app that allows learners to understand steganography basics, practice with guided exercises, encode and decode a hidden message in an image, and validate their knowledge through a quiz with score tracking. The whole experience had to be clear enough for classroom presentation, backend-free, and based on simple local logic that is easy to launch and demonstrate.
stegano.approach: The project is built with React, TypeScript, and Vite, and structured into 4 pedagogical pages: Course (definitions, LSB logic, steganography vs cryptography, PNG vs JPEG constraints), Lab (multi-step progression to manipulate bits and decode hidden messages step by step), Tools (image encode/decode area for concrete use cases), and Quiz (10-question MCQ with correction and local score tracking). The technical core is a deliberately readable implementation of LSB (Least Significant Bit): message bits are injected into the least significant bit of pixels (red channel), which minimally impacts visual quality; the decoder reads those bits back in the same order to reconstruct the message, using an end marker to know where to stop. Real-world constraints are handled: PNG is recommended (lossless), JPEG is discouraged (lossy compression can destroy hidden bits), and lab progress plus quiz scores are persisted in localStorage.
features: Educational course on steganography and binary logic applied to images
Progressive hands-on lab from theory to practice
Message encoding tool for PNG images
Decoding tool for previously encoded images
10-question quiz with detailed corrections
Local attempt history (best score, average, recent scores)
Local tracking of lab progress and quiz scores via localStorage
stegano.learnings: This project taught me how to turn a technical topic into a clear educational path for a classroom, structure a simple but complete product experience (theory, practice, assessment), explain steganography vs cryptography in concrete terms, and integrate real image-format constraints (PNG/JPEG) into implementation choices. On the process side, it strongly reinforced how to work effectively with AI on an end-to-end project without losing technical control: provide the right context, frame pedagogical goals, request precise iterations, validate technical coherence, fix what does not hold up, and keep a product-level vision from start to finish. AI can dramatically accelerate production, but final quality still depends on how well you prompt, verify, and decide.

api-auth.title: API Auth Favorites
api-auth.short: A secure REST API with JWT authentication, bcrypt hashing, and protected routes.
api-auth.desc: An API built "from scratch" to demystify modern authentication. A personal initiative born from the desire to look under the hood and truly understand the JWTs, hashing, and middlewares I saw passing by at work.
api-auth.context: This project stemmed from pure developer curiosity. While working, I often saw concepts like JWT tokens or hashed passwords fly by, but always from a distance, without having the chance to implement them myself from A to Z. Rather than staying in the dark, I wanted to tackle the subject head-on during my free time. The goal wasn't to meet an academic requirement, but simply to understand how the engine of modern web authentication runs.
api-auth.objective: The goal was to master the lifecycle of secured data: why and how do we "salt" a password (bcrypt)? How is a token (JWT) actually structured? And above all, how do we transmit it smoothly from client to server. To validate the experiment, I made an unusual choice: instead of just querying the API via Postman (a tool I already knew well), I coded a small HTML/JS interface connected to the backend. Why? To simulate a realistic Front ↔ Back testing environment, and especially to have something visually comprehensible and less austere to present in the screenshots of this portfolio!
api-auth.approach: I wanted to adopt a pragmatic and realistic method. My starting point was the official documentation of the tools (Node, Express, bcrypt, jsonwebtoken). When that wasn't enough, I supplemented this learning with targeted web tutorials and by using AI as a debugging partner. The core backend architecture relies on a clear separation: database management (SQLite), route definition, and the injection of an authentication middleware to intercept and analyze headers.
features: Registration with bcrypt password hashing (salt + hash)
Login with signed JWT token generation (24h TTL)
Protected route /api/users/profile with Bearer token verification
Reusable and injectable authentication middleware
Lightweight serverless SQLite database
Custom graphical HTML/JS interface for requests instead of Postman
HTTP header security with Helmet
api-auth.learnings: Technically, the biggest moment of clarity was understanding that a JWT is absolutely not encrypted, it's only signed. The Payload is readable by anyone, but impossible to modify without possessing the server's signature key. Professionally, the impact is huge. Even if I don't code authentication systems every day at Equans, mastering these basic concepts now allows me to effectively read error logs, spot where a network block is coming from, and have much more qualified discussions with my fellow developers.

poke-api.title: Pokémon API
poke-api.short: Introduction to REST APIs: consuming PokéAPI and building a custom Strapi API connected to a React front-end.
poke-api.desc: My first deep dive properly framed into the back-end. A project designed to demystify the concept of APIs I kept seeing at work, using the familiar Pokémon universe to bridge a Headless CMS (Strapi) with a React interface.
poke-api.context: During my apprenticeship, I was immediately thrown into the "deep end" of APIs, backend architecture, and the complex links between the front-end and the server that I didn't master at all. Everything was moving fast, and it was often confusing. This school project arrived at the perfect time: it was finally an opportunity to learn "in peace," taking the time to break down how an HTTP request works and what an API truly is. Anchoring this around the Pokémon universe made it incredibly playful: as a fan, I was much more invested in the process.
poke-api.objective: The goal wasn't just to make pretty cards. It was about making two distinct worlds communicate: a back-end (managed via Strapi) that would store our Pokémon (Pikachu, Charizard...), and a front-end (React) that would fetch and display this information. The major educational objective was to visually and technically grasp what exactly travels _between_ the two when you click a button to load data or play a Pokémon's cry.
poke-api.approach: The methodology was highly progressive. Even before touching Strapi, we started by manually hand-coding API calls to demystify HTTP syntax (GET/POST verbs, headers...). Afterwards, using Strapi v5 allowed us to visually model the database (SQLite), which removed the dryness of pure code and made endpoint generation feel almost magical. On the front-end, the React interface grabbed this raw data and displayed it. The 'Network' tab in my web inspector quickly became my best friend to trace requests in real-time.
features: Pokémon grid of cards (name, HP, sprite, type(s))
Conditional type display depending on whether a Pokémon has one or two types
Pokémon cry playback via native Web Audio API (.ogg)
Full CRUD API on /api/pokemons (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
Lightweight SQLite database, no external server required
API testing via requests.http file (VS Code REST Client)
poke-api.learnings: The real 'aha' moment came thanks to my Pokémon expertise! When doing a fetch in React and watching the Network tab, I knew exactly what to expect. If I requested data for a Pikachu and received a Squirtle's stats, I instantly knew my query was wrong. Today, I see Strapi as an excellent rapid prototyping tool. However, this project also helped me understand why the approach I now use at work (with Spring Boot, Java, and rigorous generation via Swagger) is essential for securing and strictly typing data at scale compared to a Headless CMS.

bdd-streaming.title: Streaming Platform - DB Modeling
bdd-streaming.short: Complete Database Design (CDM/LDM/PDM) and advanced analytical SQL queries for a Netflix clone.
bdd-streaming.desc: A pivotal academic project done in pairs: modeling the logical brain of a Netflix-style streaming platform from scratch. What seemed like 'just an exercise with tables' turned out to be a fascinating dive into the strict architecture and business constraints that run a real VOD service.
bdd-streaming.context: Completed at MyDigitalSchool, this assignment asked us to design the database for a platform managing subscriptions, profiles (kids/adults), a catalog (movies and series), and watch histories. This project was as technically formative as it was collaboratively. I naturally took on a supervisory role in the pair: organizing task distribution from the start to avoid getting tangled up, while tackling the thorniest issues (like ternary relationships) together to ensure we both fully understood.
bdd-streaming.objective: The goal was to deliver the entire data design chain: a mathematically clean Conceptual Data Model (CDM), its translation into a Logical Data Model (relational tables), and finally the executable Physical Data Model on MySQL with its keys, uniqueness constraints, and triggers. The real challenge wasn't just storing data, but guaranteeing its absolute integrity against complex business rules (a child profile cannot view 18+ content, a user cannot have two active subscriptions, etc.).
bdd-streaming.approach: We had to solve purely logical puzzles. The main difficulty lay in the exclusive 'Movie / Episode' inheritance: a piece of content cannot be both at once. We also spent a massive amount of time forging a consistent dataset (32 users, hundreds of history logs) to test real queries. The climax was writing complex analytical SQL queries, specifically the one identifying 'binge-watching' behaviors, which required me to understand the value of self-joins to compare timestamps for the same profile.
features: Complete and rigorous CDM (14 entities, exclusive XT specialization)
Generation of relational LDM and PDM for MySQL deployment
Implementation of strict domain constraints (UNIQUE, ENUM, composite keys)
Massive and consistent test dataset created manually for business validation
13 advanced analytical queries (views, correlated subqueries, multiple joins)
Self-join on the history table to detect binge-watching sessions
bdd-streaming.learnings: There is a 'before' and an 'after' this project. Before, SQL remained an abstract concept. This project forced me to truly manipulate and understand the different JOINs, the View system, and how the database engine interprets a complex query to optimize it. Today, whether during my apprenticeship at Equans or on my personal projects, this clear vision of what is 'expensive' in a database and how data must be structured from inception dictates my backend architecture choices.

bike.title: ReCycle E-Bikes
bike.short: Premium-oriented UI/UX design for a fictional eco-friendly e-bike brand.
bike.desc: Designing a Figma landing page isn't just about drawing beautiful rectangles. For this e-bike brand project (ReCycle E-Bikes), the challenge was to meet a strict mock client brief, break away from the 'overly green' cliché for a dark and premium look, and most importantly, integrate interactivity right from the design phase.
bike.context: This individual UI/UX exercise felt like a real-world client case. We were provided with a comprehensive brief containing visual assets, a core marketing message (eco-responsibility), and competitor references. From a developer's perspective, this was the perfect playground to push Figma to its limits and have fun designing complex animations before even thinking about writing a line of code.
bike.objective: The primary goal was to deliver a complete desktop mockup for a conversion-oriented showcase website (a punchy hero, technical arguments, testimonials carousel). But for me, the underlying stake was delivering an interactive prototype. The mockup needed to 'breathe' under the cursor (hovers, transitions) to validate that the user experience would truly be satisfying once developed.
bike.approach: I started from the client's provided inspirations to outline the art direction. To avoid the cliché neon-green eco site, I opted for a dark palette enhanced with deep green accents, positioning the product as 'premium'. Next, I fully leveraged Figma's toolset: rather than delivering static screens, I built multiple component variants to materialize hover animations on product cards, carousel transitions, and the progressive appearance of header elements.
features: Dark & Premium art direction enhanced with deep green for a sophisticated eco-friendly identity
Conversion-focused structure: Hook (Hero), Numbers (Impact), Products, Social Proof
Interactive Figma prototyping: formalized hover animations, transitions, and reveals
Detailed presentation of 3 e-bike models via interactive cards
Fluid and navigable testimonials carousel built with front-end integration in mind
bike.learnings: This project was a true revelation regarding the Dev/Design relationship. Designing all these animations helped me understand why UX/UI designers always want to add more (it's visually thrilling), and why the developers behind them might sometimes complain about performance or integration time! Moreover, mastering interface design is a strategic skill for me: tomorrow, whether freelancing or in a small start-up without the budget for a dedicated UX/UI designer, I'll be fully capable of handling both the visual rendering and its technical integration from start to finish.

lkk.title: Lee Kum Kee - Redesign
lkk.short: UI/UX redesign mockup carried out by a 4-person team on Figma with a tight one-day deadline.
lkk.desc: Beyond the simple visual overhaul of a famous Asian sauce brand, this school project threw me into full-blown creative crisis management: collaborating simultaneously with 4 people on the same Figma file, with only one day to deliver a professional and cohesive result.
lkk.context: A group project with a clear client brief: modernize the Lee Kum Kee showcase website (company presentation, product catalog, recipes). However, the main constraint was time and people. With four of us on the same canvas, we had to organize ourselves with utmost urgency to avoid stepping on each other's toes. I naturally took on a 'co-lead' position with another team member to set the production pace.
lkk.objective: The challenge wasn't just working in complete isolation on individual sub-sections. We had to merge our four distinct visions into a single, seamless layout—featuring an engaging homepage, appetizing product descriptions, and clear navigation—without the client (or jury) ever perceiving a 'Frankenstein monster' effect.
lkk.approach: To survive the deadline, organization took precedence over pure design. First, we immediately set up a standalone Figma page acting as a mini 'Design System': a unified typography library and a shared color palette. While I personally focused on anticipating front-end animations (hovers, pop-ins) and product structures, I was also co-supervising the macro assembly. Combining pieces made separately demanded sacrifices: toward the end of the day, we rushed to tweak, align, and sometimes completely redo certain sections to guarantee visual uniformity.
features: Implementation of an emergency mini-Design System (fonts library, color board)
Real-time task distribution among 4 people on the same Figma file in a co-lead setup
Complete UX restructuring (Brand story, Product catalog, Recipes index)
Creation of micro-interactions geared towards the front-end phase (reveals, hovers)
Final 'de-patchworking' polish to blend four different aesthetic sensibilities into one
lkk.learnings: Collaborating on Figma is like collaborating on Git: without strict rules, it's utter chaos. This hyper-accelerated project taught me that in UI design—unlike in code where a linter stops you from committing syntax errors—a deviation in style won't break the build, but it will ruin the overall user experience. It convinced me 100% of the absolute necessity of using design tokens and a shared library from minute zero on any project.

rl-wp.title: Rocket League - Fan Site
rl-wp.short: Figma mockup for a humorous esports fan site, pre-structured in blocks for WordPress integration.
rl-wp.desc: Why design yet another generic corporate showcase website when you've just returned from the Rocket League World Championship? This project is an unapologetic fan site, packed with community inside jokes ('trolls'), but most importantly conceived from A to Z as an ecosystem of WordPress blocks.
rl-wp.context: For a WordPress integration course, we had to produce our own responsive mockup on a free topic. Still riding the hype of returning from the Rocket League Worlds, I wanted to break the monotony of classic assignments. Picking a universe I know inside out allowed me to inject humor and heavy community references, keeping me infinitely more invested than if I'd chosen a mandated topic.
rl-wp.objective: The exercise had a specific end goal: the mockup had to be easily integrable into the WordPress philosophy. The technical challenge wasn't to design unbuildable visual eccentricities, but rather to cleverly model a grid system and 'reusable blocks' (post templates, archives, hero sections) while populating them with engaging content for both desktop and mobile.
rl-wp.approach: I tackled Figma with an integrator's mindset. I designed simple UI structures that could easily be translated into CMS components. As a result, transitioning from Desktop to Mobile happened naturally and without friction. But my real pride lies in the content. I could have taken the easy route using 'Lorem Ipsum', but I wanted to see the idea through by taking on the role of copywriter: I wrote funny, fake patch notes and included game lore, making the mockup feel 'alive' before writing a single line of code.
features: Strong Esports art direction featuring deep community references
UX functionally structured in modular 'Blocks' to mimic WordPress architecture
Fully adaptive Desktop and Mobile mockups built on fluid grids
Custom-written text content ('troll' patch notes, game history lore)
Layer and component organization ready to be handed off to a Front-end Developer
rl-wp.learnings: I learned two crucial things. Technically: UI/UX design does not happen in a vacuum. Designing web pages primarily means designing component systems compatible with a CMS's rigidity. And fundamentally: refusing 'Lorem Ipsum' and injecting your own passion (gaming) into an academic assignment changes everything. When you genuinely care about the subject, you don't count the hours spent refining details, and it instantly elevates the final quality of the project.
